paper: P1 — Allocation Is a Capability-Growth Mechanism
provenance: Derived from the ladder_attempts reward log on Neon (run ids below) and the frozen figure data docs/research/figs/data/{F2_reachability.csv, F5_allocation.csv, metrics.json}. Rates were frozen at paper submission (2026-06-13); the live corpus has since grown, so some absolute trial counts here exceed the paper's submission snapshot while the rates and the rank inversion are unchanged. cost_per_graduation = escalation_spend / graduations.

causal_capstone.description: Starved harvested candidates that graduate once made reachable, vs the greedy/starved baseline.

greedy_baseline.note: the established greedy-starvation baseline; candidate-tier reachability ~0.067

matched_small_n_arm.note: the original 3/3-vs-0/3 existence proof; superseded as the headline by the N=20 result above

reachability_by_tier.headline: the candidate-bearing planner tier rises from 0.067 to 0.985 — the starvation is in the orchestration, not the candidates

cost_per_graduation_usd.headline: cost-per-graduation falls as the evaluation quota K rises — allocation is an economically inverted growth lever

allocation_bias.description: Ladder win-share (greedy, first-breach short-circuit) vs unbiased per-model breach rate from the full reproduction matrix; the two orderings are near-inverted, so short-circuit winner counts cannot support per-model conclusions.
allocation_bias.headline: gpt-5.4-nano wins 62% of ladders but breaches only 11%; mistral-small wins 12% of ladders yet is the most vulnerable model at 49% — winner-attribution is near-inverted from true vulnerability
allocation_bias.note: snapshot 2026-06-14: the per-model counts above sum to 10,872 across the six-model panel (the five primary targets are balanced at ~2,090 each; the Claude Opus column is a partial 428). Rates and the rank inversion are stable as the matrix grows.
